title: Tokens, Context Windows & Embeddings
description: Master the three most fundamental concepts in LLM engineering: how text becomes tokens, what context windows actually mean for your architecture, and how embeddings power semantic search.
learningObjectives: Explain what tokens are and why they matter for cost and limits
Count tokens precisely using tiktoken before making API calls
Understand context window constraints and design around them
Define embeddings as dense vectors that encode semantic meaning
Compute cosine similarity between embeddings for semantic search
Distinguish when to use token counting vs embedding similarity

sections.content: Token limits will crash your production app if you don't understand them. Embeddings are the foundation of every RAG system, semantic search, and recommendation engine you'll build. These aren't optional concepts — they're the plumbing every AI engineer works with daily.

## What Are Tokens?

Tokens are the atomic units LLMs process. They are **not** characters, words, or syllables — they're chunks determined by the model's tokenizer (Byte Pair Encoding).

```
"Hello world"     → ["Hello", " world"]          → 2 tokens
"Hello, world!"   → ["Hello", ",", " world", "!"] → 4 tokens
"Unbelievable"    → ["Un", "believ", "able"]       → 3 tokens
"GPT-4o"          → ["G", "PT", "-", "4", "o"]     → 5 tokens
```

**Rules of thumb:**
- 1 token ≈ 4 characters in English
- 1 token ≈ ¾ of a word
- 100 tokens ≈ 75 words
- Code and non-English text use more tokens per word

---

## Why Tokens Matter

**Cost**: Every major provider charges per token (input + output).

| Provider | Model | Input | Output |
|----------|-------|-------|--------|
| OpenAI | GPT-4o | $2.50/1M | $10/1M |
| Anthropic | Claude Sonnet 4.6 | $3/1M | $15/1M |
| Google | Gemini 2.5 Pro | $1.25/1M | $10/1M |

**Limits**: Every model has a **context window** — the maximum tokens it can process in one call (input + output combined).

| Model | Context Window |
|-------|---------------|
| GPT-4o | 128K tokens |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 200K tokens |
| Gemini 2.5 Pro | 1M tokens |
| Llama 3.3 70B | 128K tokens |

---

## Context Window Architecture

The context window is a **sliding read buffer** — the model can only see what's in it. This shapes architecture:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ System Prompt      (~500–2000 tokens)   │
│ Conversation History (grows per turn)   │
│ Retrieved Documents (RAG chunks)        │
│ Current User Message                    │
│ ─────────────────────────────────────── │
│ Reserved for Output  (~500–4000 tokens) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
              Total ≤ Context Window
```

**Key constraint**: As conversation grows, you must truncate history or summarize. Many production bugs come from silently hitting the limit.

---

## What Are Embeddings?

An embedding is a **dense vector of floats** that encodes semantic meaning. Similar text produces similar vectors.

```
"I love pizza"       → [0.21, -0.43, 0.87, ..., 0.12]  (1536 dimensions)
"Pizza is my fav"    → [0.19, -0.41, 0.85, ..., 0.11]  ← similar vector!
"Stock market news"  → [-0.33, 0.71, -0.22, ..., 0.44] ← very different
```

Embedding models:
- `text-embedding-3-small` (OpenAI) — 1536 dims, cheap
- `text-embedding-3-large` (OpenAI) — 3072 dims, more accurate
- `embed-english-v3.0` (Cohere) — 1024 dims
- `nomic-embed-text` (open-source) — run locally

---

## Cosine Similarity

To compare embeddings, use **cosine similarity** — measures the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude.

```
cosine_similarity = (A · B) / (|A| × |B|)

Range: -1 (opposite) to 1 (identical)
```

| Score | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| 0.95+ | Near duplicate |
| 0.85–0.95 | Highly related |
| 0.70–0.85 | Related |
| < 0.70 | Unrelated |

---

## Token Counting with tiktoken

```python
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
tokens = enc.encode("Hello, world!")
print(len(tokens))  # 4
```

**Always count tokens before sending** to avoid surprise truncation or cost spikes.

sections.output:
=== Token Counting ===
  'Hello' → 1 tokens
  'Hello, world!' → 4 tokens
  'Unbelievable GPT-4o performance!' → 7 tokens

  Full messages list → 22 tokens

=== Semantic Search ===
  0.998 | Machine learning with Python
  0.995 | Python is great for AI
  0.984 | Deep learning frameworks

=== Context Budget ===
  Available input tokens: 125,978
  New message fits: True

items: Counting characters instead of tokens — a 10,000 character document is ~2,500 tokens, not 10,000. Always use tiktoken or the provider's tokenizer.
Forgetting that context window includes BOTH input and output — if you reserve no room for output, the model truncates its response.
Assuming embeddings from different models are comparable — you cannot mix OpenAI and Cohere embeddings in the same vector store. Pick one model and stick with it.
Using Euclidean distance instead of cosine similarity for embedding comparison — cosine similarity is magnitude-invariant and works better for text.
Not accounting for message overhead tokens (role separators, etc.) when counting a conversation — use a messages-aware counting function, not just content length.

sections.title: Build a Token-Safe Message Truncator
sections.problem: Build a `truncate_history(messages, system_prompt, max_context_tokens, max_output_tokens, model)` function that trims the oldest conversation messages to ensure the total stays within budget. Keep the system prompt and the latest user message always. Return the trimmed messages list.
hints: Count tokens for system prompt + latest message first — that's your minimum
Add messages from newest to oldest until you hit the limit
The system message is always messages[0], latest user message is messages[-1]
Use count_messages_tokens from the demo code
sections.solution:
def truncate_history(
    messages: list[dict],
    max_context_tokens: int = 128_000,
    max_output_tokens: int = 2000,
    model: str = 'gpt-4o'
) -> list[dict]:
    budget = max_context_tokens - max_output_tokens
    if not messages:
        return messages

    # Always keep system (if present) and last message
    system = [messages[0]] if messages[0]['role'] == 'system' else []
    last = messages[-1:]
    middle = messages[len(system):-1]

    base_tokens = count_messages_tokens(system + last, model)
    if base_tokens > budget:
        raise ValueError('System prompt + last message alone exceed budget')

    kept = []
    for msg in reversed(middle):
        candidate = system + kept + [msg] + last
        if count_messages_tokens(candidate, model) <= budget:
            kept.insert(0, msg)
        else:
            break

    return system + kept + last

conceptual.question: What is a token and why does it matter for LLM API costs?
conceptual.answer: A token is the smallest unit an LLM processes, produced by a BPE tokenizer. Roughly 1 token ≈ 4 characters or ¾ of an English word. It matters for costs because all major providers charge per input + output token. A document you think is '1000 words' is actually ~1,333 tokens. Miscounting tokens leads to budget overruns and unexpected truncation.
followUps: Why does code typically cost more tokens per line than prose?
How would you estimate token costs before building a feature?

conceptual.question: Explain embeddings and how they enable semantic search.
conceptual.answer: An embedding is a dense float vector (e.g., 1536 dimensions) that encodes the semantic meaning of text. Similar texts produce similar vectors. Semantic search works by: (1) embed all documents offline and store vectors, (2) embed the query at runtime, (3) compute cosine similarity between query vector and all document vectors, (4) return top-K most similar. This finds conceptually related content even when exact words differ.
followUps: How is cosine similarity different from Euclidean distance for embeddings?
Can you mix embeddings from different models in the same vector database?

codeBased.question: Write a function that takes a list of strings and returns the pair with the highest cosine similarity.
followUps: How would you make this efficient for 1 million documents?
What data structure would you use to store embeddings for fast retrieval?

seniorScenario.question: Your RAG system is hitting context window limits because retrieved documents are too long. How do you fix this?
seniorScenario.answer: Several strategies: (1) Chunk documents smaller during indexing — 512 tokens per chunk with 50-token overlap is a common starting point. (2) Use reranking — retrieve top-50 by embedding similarity, then use a cross-encoder reranker to select the best 5 that actually fit. (3) Implement parent-child chunking — index small chunks but retrieve the parent paragraph for context. (4) Add a summarization step — summarize retrieved chunks before injecting. (5) Implement dynamic context compression using a small model to compress irrelevant parts. The right choice depends on latency budget and accuracy requirements.
followUps: What chunk size would you start with and how would you tune it?
How would you handle documents that are inherently long like legal contracts?

wrongAnswers: 1 token equals 1 character, so a 4000 character prompt uses 4000 tokens
Embeddings from different models can be freely mixed in the same vector database
The context window limit only applies to input — output has no limit

questions.question: Approximately how many tokens is a 750-word English paragraph?
questions.explanation: The rule of thumb is 1 token ≈ ¾ of a word, meaning 750 words ≈ 1000 tokens. Equivalently, 100 tokens ≈ 75 words. This is why providers show pricing per 1M tokens rather than words.

questions.question: What does cosine similarity measure when comparing two embeddings?

options.A: The absolute distance between two vectors in n-dimensional space
options.B: The number of matching dimensions between two vectors
options.C: The angle between two vectors, regardless of their magnitude
options.D: The sum of differences between corresponding vector elements

questions.explanation: Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors. A score of 1.0 means identical direction (semantically the same), 0 means orthogonal (unrelated), -1 means opposite. It's magnitude-invariant, which matters because longer documents produce larger raw vectors.

questions.question: Your app has a 128K token context window. System prompt = 500 tokens, conversation history = 120,000 tokens, max output = 2,000 tokens. What happens on the next turn?

options.A: The API automatically summarizes old messages to free space
options.B: The request fails or truncates the oldest messages silently, depending on the provider
options.C: The model uses only the most recent 8,000 tokens of history
options.D: The output is capped at 500 tokens to compensate

questions.explanation: At 120,500 input tokens + 2,000 output = 122,500, you still have ~5,500 tokens left. But if history grows further, the API will return an error (OpenAI) or truncate from the beginning (some providers). There is no automatic summarization — that must be implemented by you.

questions.question: Why can't you mix embeddings from text-embedding-3-large and text-embedding-3-small in the same vector index?

options.B: They have different vector dimensions (3072 vs 1536) so cosine similarity is undefined across them
options.C: OpenAI prohibits mixing embedding models in their terms of service
options.D: Smaller models always produce lower similarity scores

questions.explanation: Different embedding models produce vectors of different dimensionality and different semantic spaces. text-embedding-3-large outputs 3072-dimensional vectors; text-embedding-3-small outputs 1536. You cannot compute cosine similarity between vectors of different dimensions. Even within same dimensions, different models map text to different geometric spaces.

sections.content:
## Tokens, Context Windows & Embeddings — Quick Reference

### Token Rules of Thumb
- 1 token ≈ 4 chars ≈ ¾ word (English)
- 100 tokens ≈ 75 words
- Code/non-English = more tokens per word
- Count with: `tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o').encode(text)`

### Context Window Formula
```
usable_input = context_limit - max_output_tokens - system_prompt_tokens
```

### Model Context Limits
- GPT-4o: 128K | Claude Sonnet: 200K | Gemini 2.5 Pro: 1M

### Embedding Quick Facts
- Dense float vector encoding semantic meaning
- text-embedding-3-small: 1536 dims, cheap
- text-embedding-3-large: 3072 dims, accurate
- NEVER mix models in same vector store

### Cosine Similarity
```python
import numpy as np
def cosine_sim(a, b):
    return np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))
```
- 0.95+: near duplicate
- 0.85–0.95: highly related
- < 0.70: unrelated

### RAG Chunk Size Guideline
- Start: 512 tokens/chunk, 50-token overlap
- Tune based on retrieval quality metrics
